title: 10 best herbal teas for dandruff
intro_desc: Herbal teas for dandruff are infusions made from plants that can help alleviate symptoms of dandruff, such as itching, flaking, and dryness. These teas can be used as a rinse after shampooing or as a leave-in treatment. Some examples of herbal teas that can help with dandruff include chamomile, calendula, and nettle. Chamomile has anti-inflammatory properties that can soothe an itchy scalp, while calendula and nettle have antifungal and antibacterial properties that can help to balance the scalp's natural oils and reduce flaking. By using herbal teas for dandruff, individuals can improve the health of their scalp and hair, leading to a reduction in dandruff and an improvement in their overall confidence and well-being.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Roman chamomile tea helps with dandruff because it has anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that can help soothe an itchy scalp and reduce scalp inflammation. Chamomile tea also contains antioxidants that can help strengthen hair follicles and promote hair growth. The tea can be used as a rinse after shampooing, and its calming properties can help relieve stress, which is also linked to dandruff. Regular use of chamomile tea as a hair rinse can help reduce dandruff, leaving the hair soft, shiny, and moisturized.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Roman chamomile tea has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce scalp inflammation, which is often a cause of dandruff. This is due to the presence of active constituents like chamazulene and bisabolol.

Antimicrobial: Its antimicrobial properties help combat the overgrowth of fungus or bacteria on the scalp, which can contribute to dandruff. The main antimicrobial constituents include alpha-bisabolol, matricin, and chamazulene.
Anti-pruritic: Roman chamomile tea can alleviate itching, which is a common symptom of dandruff. This is because of the anti-pruritic properties of active constituents like apigenin and luteolin.

Wound healing: The wound healing properties of Roman chamomile tea help soothe and heal irritated scalp skin, reducing dandruff symptoms. This is due to the presence of flavonoids and essential oils that promote tissue regeneration and have anti-inflammatory effects.

Sedative: The sedative properties of Roman chamomile tea can help reduce stress and anxiety, which can sometimes exacerbate dandruff symptoms. This is due to the presence of apigenin, a flavonoid with known sedative effects.
remedy_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water and let it cool down to 212°F (100°C).
Add 2 teaspoons of dried Roman chamomile flowers to a tea infuser.
Pour the hot water over the chamomile flowers and steep for 5-10 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser and let the tea cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Drink 1-2 cups of the chamomile tea daily, as part of your hair care routine, to help with dandruff.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Littleleaf linden tea helps with dandruff because it contains anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties that can soothe an itchy scalp and combat fungal infections, which are often the root cause of dandruff. The tea is rich in tannins, which can help regulate sebum production and maintain the right balance of oil on the scalp. Additionally, the flavonoids present in littleleaf linden tea have been shown to have antioxidant properties, which can help reduce oxidative stress and promote healthy hair growth. By using littleleaf linden tea as a rinse or in a hair mask, you can help alleviate dandruff symptoms and promote a healthy scalp.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Littleleaf linden tea helps reduce inflammation, which can alleviate symptoms of dandruff such as itching and redness, due to its active constituents, including flavonoids and volatile oils.
Antimicrobial: Its antimicrobial properties, attributed to the presence of tannins, help control the growth of malassezia, a fungus linked to dandruff development.
Antioxidant: The high antioxidant content, provided by flavonoids, aids in neutralizing free radicals, promoting scalp health and reducing oxidative stress, which can contribute to dandruff.
Emollient: Littleleaf linden tea has emollient properties that help moisturize and soothe the scalp, reducing dryness and flakiness, thanks to its mucilaginous constituents.
Sedative: Its sedative properties, resulting from compounds like tiliroside, can help alleviate stress and anxiety, which may indirectly contribute to dandruff management.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 2 teaspoons of dried littleleaf linden flowers and place them in a tea infuser or teapot.
Boil 8 ounces of water and pour it over the herbs, allowing them to steep for 10-15 minutes.
Cover the teapot or infuser to retain the heat and essential oils.
After steeping, remove the tea infuser or strain the tea to remove the herbs.
Drink the tea once or twice a day, or use it as a rinse for your hair after shampooing to help with dandruff.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm tea helps with dandruff because it has antifungal and antimicrobial properties that can help combat the overgrowth of malassezia, a type of fungus that can cause dandruff. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties can also help soothe an itchy, flaky scalp. Additionally, lemon balm is rich in antioxidants, which can help promote healthy hair growth and improve the overall condition of the hair and scalp. Drinking lemon balm tea or using it as a rinse after shampooing can provide relief from dandruff and leave the hair feeling fresh and revitalized.
remedy_properties: Antifungal properties: Lemon balm tea contains compounds such as citral and geraniol, which have been shown to inhibit the growth of Malassezia, a fungus that can contribute to dandruff.
Anti-inflammatory properties: Lemon balm tea has anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce scalp inflammation and irritation often associated with dandruff.

Antioxidant properties: Lemon balm tea is rich in antioxidants, such as rosmarinic acid and caffeic acid, that can help protect the scalp from oxidative stress and damage, promoting a healthier scalp environment.
Calming properties: Lemon balm tea has calming and soothing effects, which can help alleviate itchiness and discomfort caused by dandruff.

Antimicrobial properties: Lemon balm tea contains compounds with antimicrobial properties, which can help maintain a balanced scalp microbiome and prevent the overgrowth of harmful bacteria that can exacerbate dandruff symptoms.
remedy_recipe: In a small pot, boil 2 cups of water for 5 minutes.
Add 2 tablespoons of fresh or dried lemon balm leaves to the boiling water, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Remove the pot from heat, cover, and let it steep for an additional 10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the lemon balm leaves.
Drink the tea while warm, 1-2 times a day for best results.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Horsetail tea helps with dandruff because it is rich in silica and other minerals that promote healthy hair growth and scalp health. The silica content helps to strengthen hair follicles, reduce hair breakage, and improve the overall condition of the hair. Additionally, horsetail tea has anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that can help to soothe an itchy, flaky scalp and reduce the buildup of dead skin cells that can lead to dandruff. By promoting healthy hair growth and improving scalp health, horsetail tea can help to prevent and treat dandruff naturally.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Horsetail tea's anti-inflammatory properties can help reduce scalp irritation and redness associated with dandruff, thanks to the presence of phenolic acids and flavonoids.

Antimicrobial: Horsetail tea's antimicrobial properties can help control the overgrowth of Malassezia, a fungus linked to dandruff, due to the presence of antifungal compounds like silica and saponins.

Antioxidant: Horsetail tea's high antioxidant content can help protect the scalp from oxidative stress and inflammation, reducing dandruff symptoms, as a result of the presence of phenolic acids and flavonoids.
Astringent: Horsetail tea's astringent properties can help regulate sebum production, preventing a greasy scalp that can lead to dandruff, due to the presence of tannins.

Exfoliating: Horsetail tea's exfoliating properties can help remove dead skin cells from the scalp, reducing dandruff buildup, thanks to the presence of silica, which strengthens hair and promotes cell turnover.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 2 teaspoons of dried horsetail herb.
Boil 8 ounces of water and let it cool for 1-2 minutes.
Steep the horsetail in hot water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the herb particles.
Drink the tea or use it as a hair rinse, twice a day for best results.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Stinging nettle tea helps with dandruff because it contains anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that can help soothe a dry, itchy scalp and combat any potential fungal issues that may be contributing to the dandruff. Additionally, it is rich in vitamins and minerals, such as vitamin A, vitamin C, and iron, which can support healthy hair growth and improve the overall condition of the hair and scalp. The tea can be used as a rinse after shampooing or can be consumed as a beverage to promote overall scalp and hair health.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Stinging nettle tea helps reduce scalp inflammation, which can alleviate dandruff symptoms, due to the presence of compounds like lectins and flavonoids.
Antimicrobial: The tea inhibits the growth of fungi and bacteria responsible for dandruff, thanks to its tannins and essential oils.

Antioxidant: Stinging nettle tea contains antioxidants that support scalp health and reduce oxidative stress, which can exacerbate dandruff, owing to its high levels of vitamins A, C, and E, as well as minerals like selenium and zinc.
Sebum regulation: The tea helps balance sebum production, preventing excessive oiliness and dandruff, by providing sterols, fatty acids, and phytosterols.

Nutrient-rich: Stinging nettle tea is packed with vitamins and minerals that promote hair and scalp health, such as biotin, potassium, calcium, and magnesium, which contribute to overall dandruff relief.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 handfuls of fresh stinging nettle leaves, using gloves to handle the plants and avoid skin irritation.
Rinse the nettle leaves thoroughly under cold running water, then let them air dry for 10-15 minutes.
Coarsely chop the nettle leaves and place them in a heatproof pot or teapot.
Pour 4 cups of boiling water over the chopped nettle leaves, cover the pot, and let it steep for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the used nettle leaves, and enjoy it 2-3 times a day for dandruff relief.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Dandelion tea helps with dandruff because it is rich in vitamins and minerals that promote healthy hair and scalp. It contains anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce scalp inflammation and irritation, which are often associated with dandruff. Dandelion tea is also a natural diuretic, which helps to eliminate toxins from the body and improve overall health. This can lead to a healthier scalp and reduced dandruff. Additionally, dandelion tea has antibacterial properties that can help to combat the overgrowth of fungus or bacteria on the scalp, which can contribute to dandruff. Drinking dandelion tea or using it as a rinse can help to soothe the scalp, reduce dandruff, and promote healthy hair growth.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Dandelion tea can help reduce inflammation associated with dandruff, soothing the scalp and alleviating irritation. This property is due to the presence of flavonoids and phenolic acids.

Antimicrobial: The antimicrobial properties of dandelion tea help control the overgrowth of Malassezia, a fungus responsible for dandruff. Sesquiterpene lactones, such as taraxinic acid, contribute to this antimicrobial effect.

Antioxidant: Dandelion tea contains high levels of antioxidants that help protect the scalp from oxidative stress and damage, promoting a healthy scalp environment. The antioxidant properties are attributed to phenolic compounds, including chicoric and chlorogenic acids.

Detoxification: Dandelion tea supports the body's natural detoxification processes, helping to eliminate toxins that can contribute to dandruff. The detoxification properties are attributed to taraxasterol and taraxacin.

Nutrient-rich: Dandelion tea is rich in vitamins and minerals, such as vitamin A, vitamin C, and iron, which support overall scalp health and help maintain a balanced scalp ecosystem. These nutrients contribute to cell regeneration and collagen production, promoting a healthy scalp and reducing dandruff.
remedy_recipe: Gather 2-3 tablespoons of fresh dandelion flowers and leaves, ensuring they are free from pesticides and other contaminants.
Rinse the dandelion parts under cold running water, then let them air dry or pat gently with a clean towel.
Coarsely chop the dried dandelion flowers and leaves, then place them in a teapot or heat-resistant container.
Pour 16 ounces of boiling water over the chopped dandelion parts, cover the teapot or container, and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the spent dandelion pieces, and enjoy the herbal dandelion tea warm, up to three times a day for dandruff relief.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Rosemary tea helps with dandruff because it has antifungal and anti-inflammatory properties that can soothe an itchy scalp and reduce dandruff. The tea is made from the leaves of the rosemary plant, which contain compounds like carnosol and rosmarinic acid that can help to combat the overgrowth of malassezia, a type of fungus that can cause dandruff. Additionally, rosemary tea can help to improve circulation in the scalp, promoting healthy hair growth and reducing inflammation. By using rosemary tea as a rinse or adding it to your shampoo, you can help to naturally treat dandruff and maintain a healthy scalp.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Rosemary tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce scalp inflammation, which is often a cause of dandruff. The active constituents responsible for this property include carnosol and carnosic acid.

Antimicrobial: The antimicrobial properties of rosemary tea can help control the overgrowth of Malassezia, a fungus that can contribute to dandruff. The main active constituents responsible for this property are eucalyptol, alpha-pinene, and camphor.

Antioxidant: Rosemary tea contains antioxidants that can help protect the scalp from damage and reduce oxidative stress, which can exacerbate dandruff symptoms. The primary active constituents responsible for this property are rosmarinic acid and caffeic acid.

Scalp stimulation: Rosemary tea can help stimulate blood flow to the scalp, promoting hair growth and reducing dandruff symptoms. The active constituents responsible for this property include 1,8-cineole and α-pinene.
Sebum regulation: Rosemary tea can help regulate sebum production, which can contribute to dandruff. The active constituents responsible for this property include Ursolic acid and oleanolic acid.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, boil 2 cups of water for 5 minutes.
Add 2 tablespoons of fresh rosemary leaves and steep for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the rosemary leaves.
Allow the tea to cool down to a comfortable temperature.
Drink the tea once or twice a day, as needed, to help with dandruff.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Sage tea helps with dandruff because it has antifungal and antioxidant properties that can help to soothe an itchy scalp and reduce flakiness. The tea is made from the leaves of the sage plant, which contain compounds called phenolic acids and flavonoids that have been shown to have antimicrobial effects. These properties can help to combat the overgrowth of the Malassezia yeast, which is a common cause of dandruff. Additionally, sage tea can help to regulate sebum production, reducing excess oil on the scalp that can contribute to dandruff. By using sage tea as a rinse or in a hair mask, you can help to promote a healthy scalp and reduce the symptoms of dandruff.
remedy_properties: Antifungal properties: Sage tea helps with dandruff by inhibiting the growth of Malassezia, a fungus responsible for dandruff, due to the presence of camphor, tannins, and essential oils.
Anti-inflammatory properties: Sage tea reduces scalp inflammation caused by dandruff, providing relief from itching and irritation, owing to the presence of rosmarinic acid and various flavonoids.
Antioxidant properties: Sage tea helps protect the scalp from oxidative stress and damage, promoting a healthy scalp environment, as it contains high levels of phenolic acids and flavonoids.

Antiseptic properties: Sage tea helps prevent and treat scalp infections that can exacerbate dandruff by destroying harmful bacteria and promoting wound healing, thanks to the presence of thujone, camphor, and 1,8-cineole.
Astringent properties: Sage tea helps regulate sebum production and tighten skin pores, reducing excess oiliness on the scalp, due to the tannins and volatile oils found in the plant.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, boil 1 cup of water for every 1-2 teaspoons of dried sage leaves, depending on desired strength.
Add the dried sage leaves to the boiling water, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the sage leaves, pouring the liquid into a cup.
Allow the sage tea to cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Drink 1-2 cups of sage tea daily, gently massaging it into the scalp to help reduce dandruff.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Thyme tea helps with dandruff because it has antiseptic and antifungal properties that can help to combat the overgrowth of malassezia, a type of fungus that can cause dandruff. Thyme tea can also help to soothe an itchy scalp and reduce inflammation, which can help to alleviate the symptoms of dandruff. Additionally, thyme contains a compound called thymol, which has been shown to have antimicrobial properties, making it effective at fighting the bacteria and fungus that can contribute to dandruff. Drinking thyme tea or using it as a rinse after shampooing can help to reduce dandruff and promote a healthy scalp.
remedy_properties: Antifungal properties: Thyme tea has strong antifungal properties, important for dandruff as it helps combat the overgrowth of Malassezia, a fungus responsible for dandruff. The active constituents responsible are thymol and carvacrol.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Thyme tea has anti-inflammatory properties, reducing inflammation caused by dandruff and soothing an itchy scalp. The active constituents responsible for this property are flavonoids and volatile oils.

Antioxidant properties: Thyme tea contains powerful antioxidants, which can help protect the scalp from damage and reduce oxidative stress, a contributing factor in dandruff. The active constituents responsible include rosmarinic acid, luteolin, and thymonin.

Antibacterial properties: Thyme tea has antibacterial properties, which can help maintain a healthy scalp environment by limiting bacterial growth. The active constituents responsible include thymol and carvacrol.

Scalp stimulation: Thyme tea can help stimulate blood flow to the scalp, promoting hair growth and supporting a healthy scalp. The active constituents responsible for this property include ursolic acid and oleanolic acid.
remedy_recipe: In a small pot, boil 2 cups of water for 5 minutes.
Add 2 tablespoons of dried thyme leaves to the boiling water, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the thyme leaves.
Allow the tea to cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Drink the thyme tea once a day, preferably before bedtime, for best results in managing dandruff.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marjoram tea helps with dandruff because it contains antifungal and antiseptic properties that can help to combat the overgrowth of fungus and bacteria on the scalp, which can cause dandruff. Additionally, marjoram tea is rich in essential nutrients, such as vitamins A and C, calcium, and potassium, which can help to nourish and strengthen the hair follicles, promoting healthy hair growth and reducing dandruff. Furthermore, the anti-inflammatory properties of marjoram tea can help to soothe an itchy and irritated scalp, providing relief from the discomfort associated with dandruff.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Marjoram tea's anti-inflammatory properties, due to its richness in flavonoids and phenolic acids, can help soothe an itchy scalp and reduce redness associated with dandruff.
Antifungal: The antifungal properties of marjoram tea, attributed to its terpene content, can help combat the overgrowth of Malassezia, a fungus that contributes to dandruff.

Antioxidant: The high antioxidant content in marjoram tea, provided by rosmarinic and caffeic acids, can help protect the scalp from oxidative stress and strengthen hair follicles, reducing dandruff symptoms.
Antibacterial: Marjoram tea's antibacterial properties, derived from its essential oils, can help maintain a healthy scalp environment and prevent bacterial infections that may exacerbate dandruff.

Circulation-boosting: Marjoram tea's ability to improve circulation, thanks to its carvacrol and ursolic acid content, can promote nutrient delivery and waste removal in the scalp, supporting a healthy scalp and reducing dandruff.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, boil 1 cup of water for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried marjoram leaves to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let the mixture simmer for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the marjoram leaves.
Allow the tea to cool down to a drinkable temperature before consuming.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat dandruff with herbal teas is by using chamomile or green tea. Both teas have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties that can soothe an itchy scalp and help reduce dandruff. To use, steep the tea in hot water, let it cool, and then rinse your hair with it after shampooing. For chamomile tea, you can also use the cooled tea as a final hair rinse and leave it on for 10-15 minutes before rinsing it out. Repeat this process once or twice a week for best results.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to dandruff that are treated with herbal teas include seborrheic dermatitis and psoriasis. These conditions can cause itchiness, redness, and flaking of the scalp, similar to dandruff. Herbal teas, such as chamomile and nettle, have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that can help alleviate these symptoms. Chamomile tea can soothe the scalp and reduce inflammation, while nettle tea can help regulate sebum production and promote hair growth. To use, steep the tea in hot water, let it cool, and then use it as a rinse after shampooing. It's important to note that while herbal teas can help alleviate symptoms, they should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment. Always consult with a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment.
